Name: jacobian_stability_layer_routing
Title: Jacobian Spectral Routing: Training-Free Token-Adaptive Layer Skipping via Bounded Perturbation Propagation
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local Jacobian spectral norm of transformer hidden states serves as a rigorous, uncertainty-quantified proxy for layer-skip safety, enabling token-level routing decisions that provably bound downstream perturbation growth while respecting strict wall-clock latency budgets. Specifically, tokens with low Jacobian spectral norms (< ρ threshold) can safely skip subsequent layers with bounded activation drift, while high-norm tokens traverse full depth. This setting is essential because: (1) existing early-exit methods rely on task-specific training or expensive calibration, (2) prior spectral work (SQR, geometric filtering) focus on precision/noise but not depth adaptation, (3) PAC-Bayes bounds on Lipschitz propagation remain underexploited for layer routing, and (4) token-level granularity is strictly necessary to avoid layer-uniform skipping which discards fine-grained computational flexibility. The Jacobian proxy is optimal here because it directly measures sensitivity to perturbations without requiring covariance matrix inversion or cross-layer diagnostics.
Related Work: Early-exit methods (BERTweet, ALBERT) require task-specific training or labeled validation sets. DeeBERT and recent adaptive-depth work use learned routing heads, adding parameters and calibration overhead. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) uses activation covariance condition numbers for precision allocation but does not address depth. Geometric Signal Separation applies PCA filtering for noise cancellation in ICL but targets signal isolation, not layer routing. Token-level routing via learned classifiers (e.g., in DistilBERT variants) requires supervised training. Our contribution uniquely combines: (1) Jacobian spectral norms as training-free layer-skip proxies, (2) PAC-Bayes-derived perturbation bounds that formally certify skip safety, (3) single-pass, amortized spectral estimation (via power iteration) with bounded FLOPs, (4) explicit latency accounting and Pareto frontier mapping, (5) no training, no calibration set requirement, and (6) modular design compatible with any transformer architecture. We distinguish from concurrent work by anchoring routing in optimization stability theory rather than learned thresholds or task-specific calibration.
Abstract: Adaptive layer skipping offers significant latency reduction for transformer inference, yet existing methods rely on expensive training, calibration, or learned routing heads that generalize poorly across domains. We propose Jacobian Spectral Routing (JSR), a training-free framework that routes tokens adaptively through transformer layers based on local Jacobian spectral norms, which quantify hidden-state sensitivity to perturbations. Our key insight is that tokens with low spectral norms occupy well-conditioned subspaces where subsequent layer computations are redundant; skipping these layers introduces bounded, provably safe activation drift. We derive PAC-Bayes-style perturbation bounds linking Jacobian spectral decay to maximum allowable skip depth, enabling rigorous layer-routing decisions without task-specific training. To enforce strict latency budgets, we amortize spectral estimation via single-pass power iteration (O(d) FLOPs per token, negligible overhead), integrate routing thresholds inline during forward passes, and map throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers across structured latency budgets. Experiments on GLUE, SQuAD, and long-context tasks demonstrate that JSR achieves 1.2–1.8× speedup with <1% accuracy drop, outperforming learned early-exit baselines while remaining training-free and domain-agnostic. We provide deterministic latency telemetry, multi-seed variance quantification, and open-source artifacts to ensure reproducibility and deployment readiness.

Experiments.name: Jacobian Spectral Norm Estimation and Validation
Experiments.description: Implement single-pass power iteration to estimate the top Jacobian singular value σ₁(J_ℓ) = max‖h‖=1 ‖J_ℓ h‖ for each layer ℓ and token position. Validate against full SVD (gold standard) on a held-out 5% subset of GLUE dev sets. Measure estimation error (relative L2 norm) and amortized FLOPs per token. Hypothesis: power iteration converges in 2–3 iterations with <5% error and <0.1% latency overhead.

Experiments.name: Perturbation Bound Empirical Calibration
Experiments.description: For a range of Jacobian spectral thresholds ρ ∈ {0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.1, 1.3}, measure the actual downstream activation drift Δh_ℓ' = ‖h_ℓ(skip) - h_ℓ(full)‖₂ when skipping layer ℓ. Fit empirical Lipschitz constants and compare against PAC-Bayes-derived upper bounds: Δh_ℓ' ≤ C·ρ·‖h_ℓ‖₂. Plot empirical vs. theoretical bounds across 50 GLUE examples per task. Hypothesis: empirical drift tracks theoretical bounds with <20% slack.

Experiments.name: Token-Level Layer Routing Decisions
Experiments.description: Implement online routing: for each token at layer ℓ, compute σ₁(J_ℓ) via power iteration. If σ₁(J_ℓ) < ρ_threshold, skip to layer ℓ+k (k ∈ {1,2,3}). Threshold ρ is calibrated offline to target latency budget (e.g., 30% reduction). Measure per-token skip rates, layer-wise skip distribution, and skip consistency across similar tokens. Hypothesis: >40% of tokens skip at least one layer without exceeding perturbation budget.
metrics: Skip consistency (Jaccard similarity across similar contexts)

Experiments.name: Accuracy-Latency Pareto Frontier Mapping
Experiments.description: Sweep ρ_threshold across [0.3, 1.5] and measure task accuracy (GLUE, SQuAD F1) vs. wall-clock latency on a single GPU (A100 40GB). For each threshold, run 3 seeds with distinct random token orderings to quantify variance. Plot Pareto frontiers and compare against: (i) uniform layer skipping (baseline), (ii) learned early-exit (DistilBERT, DeeBERT if available), (iii) random token-level skipping. Hypothesis: JSR achieves 1.2–1.8× speedup at <1% accuracy drop, dominating baselines.

Experiments.name: Robustness to Distribution Shift
Experiments.description: Evaluate JSR on out-of-domain tasks: (i) GLUE→SQuAD transfer, (ii) in-domain GLUE→out-of-domain adversarial inputs (typos, paraphrases), (iii) long-context extrapolation (2x, 4x context length). For each shift, measure accuracy drop and latency stability. Hypothesis: JSR maintains <2% accuracy loss and <10% latency variance under domain shift, outperforming learned routers.
metrics: Stability radius (max perturbation magnitude before >1% accuracy loss)

Experiments.name: Ablation: Spectral Proxy Comparison
Experiments.description: Compare Jacobian spectral norm against alternative proxies: (i) activation norm ‖h_ℓ‖₂, (ii) layer-wise gradient norm ‖∇_h loss‖₂, (iii) Fisher information diagonal max(diag(F)), (iv) activation entropy. Measure skip rate, accuracy, and latency for each proxy under identical latency budgets. Hypothesis: Jacobian spectral norm outperforms baselines by 5–10% on accuracy-latency frontier.

Experiments.name: Amortization and Memory Analysis
Experiments.description: Profile memory usage and FLOPs for power iteration: measure peak activation memory, intermediate vector storage, and total FLOPs. Verify that amortized cost per token is <1% of layer computation. Run on models of varying sizes (BERT-base, RoBERTa-large, GPT-2-medium). Hypothesis: overhead remains <0.5% across model scales.
metrics: FLOPs per token (relative to layer FLOPs %)

Experiments.name: Multi-Seed Variance Quantification
Experiments.description: Run all experiments (GLUE, SQuAD) across 5 random seeds with distinct initialization and data shuffling. Report mean, std dev, and 95% confidence intervals for all accuracy and latency metrics. Verify reproducibility by publishing exact hardware specs, random seeds, and raw telemetry.
metrics: Coefficient of variation (CV) for accuracy and latency

Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Single-pass power iteration may exhibit high variance on poorly-conditioned layers, leading to unreliable routing decisions. Mitigation: implement Chebyshev polynomial acceleration and validate convergence diagnostics; if variance exceeds 10%, fallback to skip=false for that token.

JSR requires offline calibration of ρ_threshold to match target latency budgets. Suboptimal thresholds may yield poor accuracy-latency trade-offs. Mitigation: sweep thresholds on a small validation set (5% of dev data); provide lookup tables for common latency targets (10%, 20%, 30% reduction).

Jacobian spectral properties may vary significantly across transformer variants (BERT, GPT, T5, Vision Transformers). Bounds derived for one architecture may not transfer. Mitigation: test on ≥3 distinct architectures; report architecture-specific calibration requirements.

Attention patterns change dramatically with context length; Jacobian spectral norms estimated on short contexts may not predict skip safety on long contexts. Mitigation: evaluate explicitly on 2× and 4× context lengths; measure skip-rate drift and accuracy degradation.

Some tasks (e.g., SQuAD span extraction, token classification) may be more sensitive to layer skipping than others (e.g., classification). Hypothesis-level risk: if accuracy drops exceed 2% on any task, JSR may not be broadly deployable. Mitigation: report per-task results; identify task families where JSR is unsafe.

GPU latency measurements are subject to system noise, kernel scheduling variance, and cache effects. Wall-clock overhead claims may be inflated if not carefully controlled. Mitigation: run ≥100 iterations per configuration; report 95th percentile and coefficient of variation; use CUDA events for precise timing.
Proposal focuses on transformers; applicability to CNNs, RNNs, or hybrid architectures remains unclear. Mitigation: scope proposal explicitly to transformers; note this as future work.

Learned early-exit methods (DeeBERT, DistilBERT) may have been optimized for different latency targets or hardware. Direct comparison may be unfair. Mitigation: re-implement baselines under identical latency budgets; report both methods' accuracy-latency frontiers on the same hardware.
